teamObjective.explanation: Partner (player 2) holds only 2 cards, indicating they are near finishing. Leading the lowest non-trump card lets partner potentially win the trick with a higher card or trump and continue toward emptying their hand, while preserving our slightly stronger diamonds.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner is likely close to going out given the public hand count of 2 cards; they may be able to beat a low diamond lead, but their exact ranks and suits are unknown.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents have not played any cards, so their exact holdings are unknown. They may have higher diamonds or trumps that can take this trick, but a low lead minimizes the information and value I expose.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Diamond 3 is the lowest legal lead, giving partner the best chance to play over it and giving away the least card value if an opponent wins the trick.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Leading the 6 now burns our strongest non-trump card for little gain on the opening trick; if an opponent or partner can still beat it, we lose our best future diamond winner.
The 5 is more likely to be beaten than the 6 and still sacrifices a card that could win a later trick if lower diamonds are led.
The 4 is only slightly stronger than the 3; leading the 3 is strictly lower and therefore a safer probe that preserves every potentially useful higher card.

risks: An opponent may play a higher diamond or any trump and seize the lead.
Partner may be unable to beat the 3, leaving our team without immediate control.
Even if partner wins the trick, their remaining card(s) may not allow them to finish.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the lowest possible card, I limit the value lost when opponents take the trick and maximize the chance that partner's unknown cards are strong enough to take control.
